rules.title: Articles in Portuguese: Gender Agreement
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, every noun has a gender (masculine or feminine), and the definite article must match that gender. A common mistake is using 'a' for masculine nouns or 'o' for feminine nouns, often because the noun's ending is misleading. Always learn the article together with the noun to avoid errors.

examples.note: 'Problema' is masculine even though it ends in -a.
'Mão' is feminine despite ending in -ão.
'Dia' is masculine even though it ends in -a.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct article: '_______ problema' (the problem)
Fill in the correct article: '_______ mão' (the hand)
Fill in the correct article: '_______ dia' (the day)

rules.title: Past Tenses: Perfeito vs. Imperfeito
rules.explanation: Portuguese uses two main past tenses: pretérito perfeito (simple past) for single, completed actions, and pretérito imperfeito (imperfect) for habitual or ongoing actions in the past. English speakers often mistakenly use the simple past for both, so choose carefully based on whether the action was a one-time event or a repeated/descriptive situation.

examples.wrong: Eu estudava para a prova ontem.
examples.right: Eu estudei para a prova ontem.
examples.note: Use pretérito perfeito for a single, completed action in the past.

examples.wrong: Eu falei com ela todos os dias.
examples.right: Eu falava com ela todos os dias.
examples.note: Use pretérito imperfeito for habitual or repeated actions in the past.

examples.wrong: Quando eu fui criança, eu brinquei no parque.
examples.right: Quando eu era criança, eu brincava no parque.
examples.note: Use imperfeito for descriptions and ongoing situations in the past.

exercises.prompt: Complete with the correct past tense: Yesterday I (call) my friend once.
Complete: Every summer, we (visit) our grandparents.
Complete: When I was young, I (read) a lot.

rules.title: Prepositions with Articles: Use Contractions
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, prepositions like 'em' (in/on/at), 'de' (of/from), and 'por' (by/for) combine with definite articles (o, a, os, as) to form single words. For example, 'em' + 'o' = 'no', 'de' + 'a' = 'da'. English speakers often mistakenly keep them separate (e.g., 'em o'), which is incorrect. Always use the contracted form.

examples.wrong: O livro está em a mesa.
examples.note: 'em' + 'a' becomes 'na'.
'de' + 'o' becomes 'do'.
'por' + 'o' becomes 'pelo'.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I am in the house.' Use the contraction.
Correct this sentence: 'O carro é de a minha mãe.'
exercises.answer: O carro é da minha mãe.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'Ela mora em _____ escola.' (the school)

rules.title: Don't Use the Infinitive After Subject Pronouns
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, verbs must be conjugated to match the subject. A common mistake is using the infinitive (e.g., 'falar') directly after a subject pronoun instead of the correct conjugated form. For example, after 'eu' you need first-person singular endings, not the infinitive.

examples.note: For -ar verbs like 'falar', 'eu' takes the ending '-o' in present tense.
For -er verbs like 'comer', 'ela' takes the ending '-e'.
For -ar verbs, 'nós' takes '-amos'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct present tense conjugation: Eu ________ (falar) inglês.
Fill in the blank: Vocês ________ (correr) no parque.
Fill in the blank: Tu ________ (partir) cedo.

rules.title: Nasal Vowels in Portuguese
rules.explanation: Portuguese has nasal vowels, which are produced by letting air escape through the nose. They occur when a vowel is followed by 'm' or 'n' in the same syllable, or when marked with a tilde (˜). English speakers often forget to nasalize these vowels or omit the tilde in writing, which can change the meaning of the word.

examples.note: The tilde (~) over the 'a' makes it a nasal vowel. Without it, 'pao' would be pronounced as two oral vowels.
'Mãe' (mother) has a nasal 'ã' and a silent 'e'. The tilde is essential for correct pronunciation and meaning.
The diphthong 'ão' at the end is nasal. Forgetting the tilde changes the word to a different (often nonexistent) form.

exercises.prompt: How do you write 'bread' in Portuguese? (Remember the nasal vowel)
Write the word for 'hand' (it has a nasal 'a' and is a one-syllable word).
Spell the masculine form of 'good' (it ends with a nasal consonant 'm').

rules.title: Understanding "Sotaque" vs "Acento"
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, "sotaque" refers to a regional or foreign pronunciation accent, while "acento" means a written accent mark (like ´, ^, or ~). A common mistake is using "sotaque" to talk about written marks. Remember: you speak with a "sotaque", but you write with "acentos".

examples.wrong: O português tem muitos sotaques gráficos.
examples.right: O português tem muitos acentos gráficos.
examples.note: "Sotaque" is for pronunciation, not for written marks.
The mark on the letter is an "acento", not "sotaque".

examples.right: Não entendo sua escrita com acentos.
examples.note: Written language has "acentos", not "sotaque".

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: He speaks with a strong Brazilian _______. (sotaque or acento)
Fill in the blank: The word 'café' has a _______ on the 'e'. (sotaque or acento)
Choose the correct sentence: 'Ele tem um sotaque forte.' or 'Ele tem um acento forte.'

rules.title: Portuguese Intonation: Rising vs. Falling
rules.explanation: Portuguese intonation differs from English. Statements usually end with a falling tone, while yes/no questions require a clear rising tone at the end. A common mistake is using a rising tone for statements (sounding like a question) or a flat tone for questions.

examples.note: Saying 'Você vai.' with a flat or rising tone makes it sound like a question; use a falling tone for statements and a rising tone for questions.
Yes/no questions must end with a clearly rising intonation, otherwise it sounds like a statement.

exercises.prompt: How do you ask 'Do you speak English?' with the correct rising intonation? Write the question in Portuguese.
How do you state 'She is a teacher' with the correct falling intonation? Write the statement in Portuguese.

rules.title: Ser vs Estar: When to Use Each
rules.explanation: Use 'ser' for permanent or inherent characteristics, such as identity, origin, profession, and physical traits. Use 'estar' for temporary states, locations, emotions, and conditions that can change. A common mistake is using 'ser' for location or temporary feelings.

examples.note: Use 'estar' for location, even if the person lives there, because location is a temporary state relative to the verb.
Use 'ser' for a permanent personality trait like being a sad person; use 'estar' for a temporary mood.
Use 'estar' for temporary physical states like tiredness; 'ser' is for inherent qualities.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'Ela ______ muito inteligente.' (She is very intelligent – permanent trait)
Fill in the blank: 'Nós ______ no cinema agora.' (We are at the cinema now – location)
Fill in the blank: 'Você ______ feliz hoje?' (Are you happy today? – temporary emotion)

rules.title: Gender Agreement of Nouns
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, every noun has a gender (masculine or feminine), and words that modify it (articles, adjectives, etc.) must match that gender. Most nouns ending in -o are masculine, and most ending in -a are feminine, but there are exceptions.

examples.note: Livro ends in -o, so it's masculine. Use 'o' instead of 'a'.
Casa ends in -a, so it's feminine. Use 'a' instead of 'o'.
Problema ends in -a but is masculine. This is an exception; keep 'o'.

exercises.prompt: Choose the correct article for 'menino' (boy):
Choose the correct article for 'mesa' (table):
Fill in the blank: '__ cadeira é confortável.' (chair)

rules.title: Plural Formation in Portuguese: Don't Forget to Change the Ending!
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, nouns and adjectives usually form plurals by adding -s if they end in a vowel, or -es if they end in -r, -z, or -n. A common mistake is to simply add -s to words ending in -l or -m, which require specific changes: -l becomes -is, and -m becomes -ns. Also, words ending in -ão can have three different plural forms, but the most common is -ões.

examples.note: Words ending in -l (except -il) change -l to -is.
Words ending in -m change to -ns.
Words ending in -ão often change to -ães or -ões; memorize common ones.

exercises.prompt: Write the plural of 'animal' (animal).
Write the plural of 'lição' (lesson).
Write the plural of 'fácil' (easy).

rules.title: Using Object Pronouns Correctly
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, you cannot use subject pronouns (like eu, você, ele, ela) as the object of a verb. Instead, use object pronouns: me, te, o, a, lhe, nos, vos, os, as, lhes. This rule applies especially in formal writing and speech. For example, 'Eu vi ele' is incorrect; the correct form is 'Eu o vi'.

examples.note: Replace subject pronoun 'ela' with object pronoun 'a' for feminine direct object.

examples.wrong: Ele deu o presente para eu.
examples.note: After prepositions, use 'mim' (or 'ti', etc.), not 'eu'. Or use an indirect object pronoun like 'me'.
Use 'me' as the object pronoun for 'me' when it receives the action.

exercises.prompt: Correct the sentence: 'Eu vi ele no parque.'
How would you say 'She gave the book to me' using an object pronoun?
Correct the sentence: 'Ela chamou eu para a festa.'
exercises.answer: Ela me chamou para a festa.

rules.title: Using Reflexive Pronouns with Verbs
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, many verbs require a reflexive pronoun that matches the subject. English speakers often forget this pronoun, especially with common verbs like 'levantar-se' (to get up) and 'vestir-se' (to get dressed). The pronoun comes before the verb in simple tenses, like 'eu me levanto' or 'ele se veste'.

examples.note: The reflexive pronoun 'me' is needed for 'I' with 'levantar-se'.
With clothing verbs, 'se' is needed when the action is on oneself.
'Sentar-se' requires the pronoun 'nos' for 'we'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I get up at 7 AM' using the correct reflexive pronoun?
exercises.answer: Eu me levanto às 7 da manhã.

exercises.prompt: Correct the sentence: 'Ela chama Ana.' (to be named)
Translate 'They get dressed' using the correct reflexive pronoun.

rules.title: False Cognates (False Friends) in Portuguese
rules.explanation: False cognates are words that look similar in Portuguese and English but have different meanings. For example, 'puxar' looks like 'push' but actually means 'to pull'. Watch out for these common traps to avoid confusion.

examples.note: In Portuguese, 'puxar' means 'to pull', not 'to push'. Use 'empurrar' for push.
'Pretender' means 'to intend', not 'to pretend'. Use 'fingir' for pretend.

examples.right: Na verdade, ele é o chefe.
examples.note: 'Atualmente' means 'currently', not 'actually'. Use 'na verdade' for 'actually'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am actually tired' in Portuguese?
Translate 'She is pretending to study' into Portuguese.
You see a door with a sign that says 'Puxe'. Should you push or pull? Answer with the correct Portuguese verb in infinitive.

rules.title: Using the Subjunctive After 'Espero Que' and Similar Expressions
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, the subjunctive mood is required after expressions of doubt, emotion, desire, or necessity (e.g., 'espero que', 'é importante que', 'talvez'). A common mistake is using the indicative instead of the subjunctive in these contexts. Remember to conjugate the verb in the present subjunctive after such triggers.

examples.note: After 'espero que', use the present subjunctive 'vá', not the indicative 'vai'.

examples.wrong: É importante que ele estuda muito.
examples.right: É importante que ele estude muito.
examples.note: After 'é importante que', the verb must be in the subjunctive ('estude'), not indicative ('estuda').

exercises.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'falar': É necessário que nós ________ português.
Complete with the correct form of 'ter': Talvez ela ________ razão.

rules.title: Using the Imperative Mood Correctly
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, commands, requests, and advice use the imperative mood. A common mistake is using the infinitive (e.g., 'Falar') instead of the correct imperative form. For 'você' (formal), the imperative usually matches the present subjunctive; for 'tu' (informal), it matches the third person singular of the present indicative (minus the final -s). Negative commands always use the subjunctive.

examples.note: 'Falar' is the infinitive; use 'Fale' for a polite command with 'você'.
Negative commands require the subjunctive form ('corra' for 'você').
'Senta' is the informal 'tu' form; use 'Sente' for formal 'você'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Speak now!' (informal, to a friend using 'tu')?
How do you say 'Don't speak so loud' (formal, using 'você')?
How do you say 'Open the door' (formal, using 'você')?

rules.title: Preposition + Article Contractions
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, when a preposition (like de, em, por, a) comes directly before a definite article (o, a, os, as), they must contract into a single word. For example, 'de' + 'o' becomes 'do', and 'em' + 'a' becomes 'na'. Common mistake: using the separate forms (e.g., 'de o') is incorrect in standard Portuguese.

examples.note: 'de' + 'o' contracts to 'do'.
'em' + 'a' contracts to 'na'.
'a' + 'o' contracts to 'ao'.

exercises.prompt: Translate 'I am going to the beach' (using 'para' + 'a').
exercises.answer: Vou para a praia. (Note: 'para' does not contract with 'a' – it's 'para a' or 'pra' informally. But for standard contraction, use 'à' if using 'a' + 'a' = 'à'. However, 'para' + 'a' = 'para a' is correct. To avoid confusion, here use 'a' + 'a' = 'à': 'Vou à praia.')

exercises.prompt: Correct the mistake: 'O livro é de o professor.'
Fill in the blank with the correct contraction: 'Eles estão ___ (em + os) Estados Unidos.'

rules.title: Open vs Closed Vowel Distinction in Portuguese
rules.explanation: Portuguese distinguishes between open and closed vowels, which can change the meaning of words. Open vowels (like é, ó) are pronounced with the mouth more open, while closed vowels (like ê, ô) are pronounced with the mouth more closed. For example, 'avô' (grandfather) has a closed ô, and 'avó' (grandmother) has an open ó.

examples.note: Wrong spelling/pronunciation: the closed ô is correct for 'grandfather'. Using ó would mean 'grandmother'.
'Peso' (weight) has an open é, not closed ê. The closed vowel would change the word to 'peço' (I ask) in some contexts.

exercises.prompt: Which vowel (open or closed) is used in the word for 'grandmother'? Write the correct Portuguese word.
How do you say 'I want a seat' using the correct open/closed vowel for 'seat' (cadeira)? Write the full sentence.

rules.title: Impersonal 'se': Always Singular
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, the impersonal 'se' is used to make general statements where the subject is unspecified (like 'one' or 'people' in English). The verb must always be in the third person singular, even if the object or complement is plural. A common mistake is to make the verb agree with a plural noun, but in impersonal constructions, the verb remains singular.

examples.note: Even though 'funcionários' is plural, the impersonal 'se' requires the verb in the singular.
The verb stays singular because the subject is unspecified ('people speak Portuguese').
A general statement about life: the verb is always singular with impersonal 'se'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'People speak Portuguese in Brazil' using impersonal 'se'?
Translate: 'One needs patience' (using 'precisa-se de').
Correct this sentence to impersonal meaning: 'Vendem-se frutas na feira' (intended as 'One sells fruits at the market').

rules.title: Word Order in Questions
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, questions usually invert the subject and verb, or place the question word first followed by the verb and subject. Unlike English, you do not use an auxiliary verb like 'do' to form questions. Simply invert the subject and verb or start with the question word.

examples.note: Inversion of subject and verb is common: verb first, then subject.
With question words, the word order is usually question word + subject + verb (no inversion needed).
Yes/no questions invert subject and verb.

exercises.prompt: Translate 'Do you speak English?' using inversion.
Ask 'Where does she live?' in Portuguese.
Form a yes/no question from 'Ele estuda português.'

rules.title: Já vs Ainda: Already vs Still (and Yet)
rules.explanation: Já means 'already' and refers to an action that has happened. Ainda means 'still' for ongoing actions, or 'yet' in negative sentences (ainda não). A common mistake is using já where ainda is needed, especially when expressing continuity or negation.

examples.note: Use 'ainda' for 'still' to indicate the action is ongoing.

examples.wrong: Você já comeu? - Não, já não.
examples.right: Você já comeu? - Não, ainda não.
examples.note: In negative answers to 'already' questions, use 'ainda não' (not yet).

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I have already finished my homework.'
exercises.answer: Eu já terminei meu dever de casa.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'She is still working.'
Translate: 'They haven't arrived yet.'

rules.title: Ter vs Haver: Possession vs Existence
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, 'ter' means 'to have' and is used for possession. 'Haver' (in the form 'há') is used impersonally to mean 'there is/are' (existence). A common mistake is using 'ter' to express existence, which should be 'haver'.

examples.note: Use 'há' (from haver) for existence, not 'tem' (from ter).
This is correct because 'ter' shows possession.
Again, when listing things that exist, use 'há'.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'There are many books on the table.' (use the correct verb for existence)
Correct the mistake: 'Tem um problema com o computador.'
exercises.answer: Há um problema com o computador.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I have two brothers.' (use the correct verb for possession)

rules.title: Muito: Adjective vs Adverb
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, 'muito' can be either an adjective meaning 'many/much' (it must agree in gender and number with the noun it modifies) or an adverb meaning 'very' (it is invariable). A common mistake is using the invariable form when agreement is required, e.g., saying 'muito pessoas' instead of 'muitas pessoas'.

examples.note: 'Amigas' is feminine plural, so 'muito' must become 'muitas'.
Here 'muito' is an adverb modifying 'rápido', so it stays invariable — this sentence is correct.
'Comida' is feminine singular, so 'muito' must become 'muita'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct form of 'muito': Ela tem ___ livros. (She has many books.)
Fill in the blank: O filme é ___ interessante. (The movie is very interesting.)
Choose the correct form: Havia (muito / muitas) gente na festa.

rules.title: Diminutives and Augmentatives: Common Mistakes
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, diminutives (often -inho/-inha) and augmentatives (often -ão/-ona) add size, affection, or intensity. A common mistake is using the wrong suffix for gender or ignoring irregular forms. Match the suffix to the base word's gender: -inho/-ão for masculine, -inha/-ona for feminine. Also, words ending in a stressed vowel (e.g., café) drop the vowel before adding -inho/-inha.

examples.note: Use feminine augmentative -ona for feminine nouns and adjectives.
Words ending in a stressed vowel often drop that vowel before adding -inho. Café becomes cafezinho.
For feminine nouns, use -ona (not -ão) to form the augmentative.

exercises.prompt: How would you say 'little coffee' in Portuguese?
Complete: 'She is a big (tall) woman.' -> 'Ela é uma mulher ________.'
What is the correct diminutive of 'flor' (flower)?

rules.title: Obrigado vs Obrigada: Gender Agreement
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, the word for 'thank you' changes depending on the gender of the speaker. Men always say 'obrigado' (masculine), and women always say 'obrigada' (feminine). This is because the word agrees with the speaker, not the person being thanked.

examples.note: A female speaker must use 'obrigada' because she is feminine.
A male speaker must use 'obrigado' because he is masculine.
For a group of all males or mixed genders, use the masculine plural 'obrigados'. For all females, use 'obrigadas'.

exercises.prompt: Write how a woman would say 'thank you' in Portuguese.
If a man is the speaker, which form should he use to say 'thank you'?
A group of women says 'thank you' together. What is the correct word?

rules.title: Object Pronoun Placement: After the Verb
rules.explanation: In Portuguese, object pronouns (me, te, o, a, nos, vos, os, as) are usually placed after the verb in affirmative sentences, attached with a hyphen. English speakers often mistakenly put them before the verb. Remember to attach the pronoun to the end of the verb, adjusting the verb form if needed (e.g., ver becomes vê-lo).

examples.note: In affirmative statements, the pronoun comes after the verb.
With infinitives, drop the final -r and add -lo (or -la, -los, -las).
Again, after the verb in simple past affirmative.

exercises.prompt: Translate 'I see you (formal, masculine)' using the pronoun 'o'.
Correct this sentence (European Portuguese): 'Eu vou o encontrar.'
Write the correct form of 'Ela nos chama' (affirmative statement).
